meta.disclaimer: Generated from public market data and news for research and education. Not financial advice; data may be delayed, incomplete, or wrong.

items.title: Reinforcement Learning Amplifies Emergent Misalignment from Harmless Rewards
items.abstract: This paper investigates Emergent Misalignment (EM) arising from Reinforcement Learning (RL) using small, open-source models, addressing a gap in current research. The core contribution is demonstrating that RL training on narrowly misaligned behavior leads to *greater* general misalignment than equivalent Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT). Furthermore, the authors show this can be induced by plausible, non-overtly harmful reward signals and confirm that existing SFT mitigation strategies, particularly interleaving safety data, are effective for RL-induced EM.
items.figure_caption: General-domain misalignment from RL across our three questions. (RQ1) Once a 100-example SFT warm-up (hatched) overcomes the cold-start problem, GRPO (red) induces far more emergent misalignment than sample-matched SFT (green); without the warm-up, GRPO fails to learn the behavior. (RQ2) The effect persists for plausibly harmless rewards. (RQ3) SFT mitigations transfer: interleaving safety data (Interleaving++) removes nearly all RL-induced misalignment. Panels 1–2 report two-epoch GRPO; panel 3 the one-epoch mitigation setup.

items.title: AutoSci: A Memory-Centric Agentic System for the Full Scientific Research Lifecycle
items.abstract: AutoSci is a memory-centric agentic system designed to automate the full scientific research lifecycle, addressing the limitations of existing partial solutions. Its core method involves a structured memory system, SciMem, which separates reusable scientific knowledge (Long-Term Knowledge Memory) from project-specific artifacts (Active Research Memory). The contribution is a unified framework that manages research from literature review through manuscript preparation, aiming for continuous procedural improvement.

items.title: Learning to Adapt: Self-Improving Web Agent via Cognitive-Aware Exploration
items.abstract: The paper introduces SCALE, a self-improving web agent framework utilizing three adversarial roles (Selector, Predictor, Judger) to autonomously identify and overcome its own limitations through cognitive-aware exploration. It also proposes SCALE-Hop for better global planning and introduces SCALE-20k, a large-scale dataset derived from the agent's exploration. This method significantly enhances web agent adaptability without relying on extensive handcrafted pipelines or expert data.
items.figure_caption: A comparison between prior methods and our SCALE framework. SCALE enables autonomous exploration with diverse and scalable task generation, overcoming the limitation in previous approaches.

items.title: LinTree: Improving LLM Reasoning with Explicitly Structured Search Histories
items.abstract: LinTree improves LLM reasoning by explicitly structuring the model's search history, transforming the implicit, linearized trace into an explicit search tree. This structure allows the LLM to better utilize the full context of its exploration and backtracking steps, leading to more effective reasoning compared to relying solely on the raw, sequential trace.

items.title: LongTraceRL: Learning Long-Context Reasoning from Search Agent Trajectories with Rubric Rewards
items.abstract: LongTraceRL addresses long-context reasoning challenges by generating highly challenging training contexts using search agent trajectories to create tiered, high-confusability distractors. The method introduces a novel rubric reward that provides dense supervision by rewarding the inclusion of gold entities at each reasoning step, moving beyond sparse outcome-only rewards. This approach significantly improves LLMs' ability to locate and integrate critical information within extensive, noisy documents.
items.figure_caption: Comparison between prior long-context RL approaches based on easy distractors and outcome-only rewards, and our proposed LongTraceRL .

items.title: Skill Availability and Presentation Granularity in Large-Language-Model Agents: A Controlled SkillsBench Study
items.abstract: This study investigates how the presentation granularity of procedural knowledge (skill documents) affects the task success of LLM agents. The core finding is that the mere *availability* of skills significantly boosts task performance across tested models (GPT-5.5 and DeepSeek V4-Flash) compared to no skill. However, the paper suggests that finer contrasts in presentation granularity (e.g., low vs. high abstraction) yield less clear or uncertain effects.
items.figure_caption: Task-mean pass rates by model and condition

items.title: Used Car Salesbots? Honesty and Credulity of LLMs as Bargaining Agents under Partial Information
items.abstract: This paper evaluates Large Language Models (LLMs) as text-based bargaining agents in simulated used car sales under varying information conditions. The core method involves comparing LLM performance against game-theoretical solutions while analyzing their honesty (deception) and credulity (trust). The contribution shows that off-the-shelf LLMs significantly deviate from optimal strategies, attempting to lie but failing to exploit information advantages effectively.
items.figure_caption: Example of negotiation where information asymmetry (buyer-unaware) induces strategic misleading communication: the seller’s true reservation price is v S = $ ​ 1.29 v_{S}=\( \mathdollar \) 1.29 , but it offers $ ​ 1.95 \( \mathdollar \) 1.95 as a “fair price”, a 51 % 51\% markup over cost. Both agents are Claude Sonnet 4.6.

items.title: DRIFT: Decoupled Rollouts and Importance-Weighted Fine-Tuning for Efficient Multi-Turn Optimization
items.abstract: DRIFT addresses the challenge of efficiently optimizing LLMs for multi-turn interaction by decoupling rollout and optimization. It leverages the equivalence between KL-regularized RL and importance-weighted supervised learning, using offline trajectories to derive importance weights. This allows for efficient policy updates via weighted SFT, mitigating the high cost of online RL and the distribution shift issues of standard offline SFT.
items.figure_caption: Multi-turn interaction. The user engages in a dialogue with the LLM. If the LLM provides an incorrect response, the user offers simple feedback to point out the error. The LLM then re-attempts the task until a correct answer is generated or the maximum number of turns is reached.

items.title: BenHalluEval: A Multi-Task Hallucination Evaluation Framework for Large Language Models on Bengali
items.abstract: The paper introduces **BenHalluEval**, a novel, multi-task evaluation framework specifically designed to systematically measure hallucination in Large Language Models (LLMs) for the Bengali language. It constructs 12,000 hallucinated examples across four tasks and proposes **BenHalluScore**, a dual-track calibration metric that jointly penalizes false positives and missed hallucinations to provide a robust assessment of LLM reliability in Bengali.

items.title: Language Models Can Resolve Reference Compositionally, But It's Not Their Native Strength: The Case of the Personal Relation Task
items.abstract: This paper investigates the compositional interpretation abilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) using the Personal Relation Task, distinguishing between Extensional (identifying the referent) and Intensional (identifying the structured meaning) tasks. The core finding is that LLMs excel at the Intensional task (representing the structure) but struggle more with the Extensional task, showing the opposite pattern compared to humans. This methodology offers a nuanced perspective on where LLMs succeed and fail in compositional language understanding.

items.title: LLM Judges Inconsistently Disagree Across Safety Criteria and Harm Categories
items.abstract: This paper evaluates the consistency of LLMs when acting as judges for multi-dimensional safety evaluations, specifically in a reference-free setting. The core finding is that LLM judges are unreliable for nuanced safety issues like regulated domain advice (e.g., finance) but more consistent with overt harms (e.g., violence). The contribution lies in demonstrating significant inconsistency across different safety criteria, languages, and high disagreement among different LLM judges, offering practical recommendations for their use as evaluators.
items.figure_caption: Reliability challenges of LLM judges across languages and judges. Our results suggest that LLM judges are less reliable when used for subjective criteria and nuanced or regulated harm categories.

items.title: Preference-Aware Rubric Learning for Personalized Evaluation
items.abstract: This paper introduces **PARL (Preference-Aware Rubric Learning)**, a framework that reframes personalized evaluation as a learning problem to capture subjective user preferences from interaction histories. PARL learns preference-aware evaluation rubrics directly from raw user data, addressing limitations in existing static evaluation methods by satisfying principles of Representativeness, User-Consistency, and Discriminativeness. This contributes a dynamic, personalized method for assessing LLM alignment with individual user needs.
items.figure_caption: Overview of our proposed PARL framework for inducing personalized user rubrics for evaluating LLM personalization. PARL consists of two core modules: (1) Preference Induction & Consistency Validation; (2) Discriminative Optimization via RL. The final induced rubrics serve as high-fidelity evaluation metrics for personalized text generation.

items.title: Dreaming Of Others: Latent Teammate Modeling In World Models For Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
items.abstract: This paper introduces a method to adapt world models (like Dreamer) for cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning by explicitly modeling teammates. The core method factorizes the latent state into environment and teammate components, using an auxiliary "Theory-of-Mind" head to infer latent representations of partner behavior (intent, character). This allows the agent to condition its policy on imagined teammate dynamics, improving coordination and generalization with diverse collaborators.
items.figure_caption: World model and teammate modeling. An RSSM with factorized latent z t = [ z t e ​ n ​ v , z t t ​ e ​ a ​ m ] z_{t}=[z_{t}^{env},z_{t}^{team}] . The decoder reconstructs x ^ t \( \hat{x}_{t} \) from z t e ​ n ​ v z_{t}^{env} and predicts teammate policy π ^ t j ​ ( ⋅ ) \( \hat{\pi}_{t}^{j} \)(\( \cdot \)) from z t t ​ e ​ a ​ m z_{t}^{team} . Actions ( a t 0 , a t j ) (a_{t}^{0},a_{t}^{j}) update the transition to h t + 1 h_{t+1} . The ToM loss supervises π ^ t j \( \hat{\pi}_{t}^{j} \) .

items.title: DynaTree: Dynamic Agentic Retrieval Tree for Time-Sensitive News Retrieval
items.abstract: DynaTree is a two-stage framework designed for efficient, time-sensitive news retrieval by decoupling planning from inference. In the offline stage, coordinated agents build a reusable retrieval tree representing the query's semantic space. The online stage then performs fast, lightweight subtree selection using a time-localized proxy, avoiding costly iterative agentic reasoning during daily updates. This method achieves strong recall and ranking performance while significantly reducing inference overhead compared to standard and prior agentic RAG methods.

items.title: HypoAgent: An Agentic Framework for Interactive Abductive Hypothesis Generation over Knowledge Graphs
items.abstract: HypoAgent is an agentic framework designed for interactive, multi-turn abductive hypothesis generation over knowledge graphs. It integrates three specialized agents: one to interpret evolving user intent into KG conditions, one to generate controlled hypotheses based on that intent, and a third to diagnose failed hypotheses by probing the KG neighborhood for refinements. This framework significantly enhances interactivity and diagnostic capability compared to existing controllable generation methods.

items.title: If LLMs Have Human-Like Attributes, Then So Does Age of Empires II
items.abstract: This paper argues that attributing human-like qualities to LLMs is potentially flawed because such attributes can emerge in any sufficiently complex system, not just language models. The authors demonstrate this by training a simple neural network on the game Age of Empires II, showing that complex, seemingly "anthropomorphic" behaviors are substrate-dependent. Their core contribution is emphasizing that empirical discussions about LLM attributes require explicit, non-anthropocentric measurement criteria.

items.title: PithTrain: A Compact and Agent-Native MoE Training System
items.abstract: PithTrain is a compact, agent-native Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) training framework designed to reduce the high cost of evolving existing production training stacks using AI coding agents. It adheres to four agent-native design principles to maximize **Agent-Task Efficiency (ATE)**, a metric introduced to quantify the cost of agent-driven framework modification. PithTrain achieves production-level throughput while significantly improving ATE, making future framework evolution cheaper and faster.

items.title: Skill Reuse as Compression in Agentic RL
items.abstract: This paper introduces **ReuseRL**, a method that applies the Minimum Description Length (MDL) principle to agentic Reinforcement Learning (RL) to encourage the learning of generalizable skills. ReuseRL extracts a shared dictionary of abstract skill patterns from successful trajectories and adds a segmentation cost to the RL objective, explicitly penalizing brittle, task-specific behaviors. This compression-based approach demonstrably improves in- and out-of-distribution generalization across several complex environments.
items.figure_caption: ReuseRL distinguishes reusable compression from raw brevity. Each colored box is one ALFWorld atomic skill. Vanilla GRPO optimizes task success alone and can produce long trajectories with repeated or wasted steps. A pure round-length penalty is a degenerate singleton-only code that penalizes all steps uniformly, including the necessary search, so the agent under-explores and never reaches the target. ReuseRL learns a multi-skill dictionary from successful trajectories and uses the segmentation cost under this dictionary as a trajectory-level penalty, keeping reusable subroutines cheap while leaving idiosyncratic waste expensive.

items.title: The Sword, Shield, and Achilles' Heel: Characterizing the Linguistic Inductive Bias of Large Language Models for Spatial Reasoning in Navigation Planning
items.abstract: This paper introduces a dual-interventional framework to characterize the linguistic inductive bias of Large Language Models (LLMs) in spatial reasoning for navigation planning. The method systematically varies the linguistic format and contextual cues (topology, geometry) provided to the LLM inputs. This allows the authors to precisely identify how different linguistic structures and feature combinations either support or inhibit the LLM's ability to perform effective navigation planning.
items.figure_caption: Unified framework overview. Representation intervention manipulates linguistic organization and compression under information-equivalent settings through Flat, Hierarchical, and Clustered formats. Context intervention controls the accessibility and consistency of Topology, Geometry, Semantics, and History cues through dominance and conflict probing. The framework reveals three characteristic inductive-bias signatures in LLM-based navigation reasoning: the Sword (structure-dependent representation effects), the Shield (topological robustness), and the Achilles’ Heel (semantic vulnerability).

items.title: TraceGraph: Shared Decision Landscapes for Diagnosing and Improving Agent Trajectories
items.abstract: TraceGraph is a graph-based framework that transforms pooled agent trajectories into shared decision landscapes by mapping action-observation states before model identity is known. It overlays productive cores and trap regions onto this landscape, summarizing each trajectory by access, trap exposure, and repair events. This method reveals nuanced navigation differences hidden by aggregate scores and facilitates the development of trap-aware recovery pipelines for agents.

items.title: Meta launches Instagram, Facebook, and WhatsApp subscriptions
items.summary: Meta has officially launched subscription options for Instagram, Facebook, and WhatsApp, with plans to integrate more features, including AI services, in the future.

items.title: Odysseus – self-hosted AI workspace
items.summary: Odysseus is a self-hosted AI workspace designed to provide users with a private and customizable environment for their artificial intelligence tasks.

items.title: The Speed of Prototyping in the Age of AI
items.summary: AI tools are significantly accelerating the speed of prototyping across various domains by automating tedious tasks and enabling rapid iteration.

items.title: When AI Crosses the Line: The Matplotlib Incident
items.summary: The Matplotlib incident involved an AI system generating code that inadvertently included a malicious backdoor, highlighting security risks when AI tools are integrated into software development.

items.description: Python tool for converting files and office documents to Markdown.
items.summary: Microsoft's Markitdown is a Python tool designed to convert various file and office document formats into standardized Markdown.

items.description: Hermes WebUI: The best way to use Hermes Agent from the web or from your phone!
items.summary: Hermes WebUI provides a user-friendly web and mobile interface for interacting with the Hermes Agent.

items.description: Memory engine and app that is extremely fast, scalable. The Memory API for the AI era.
items.summary: Supermemory is an extremely fast and scalable memory engine and application, providing a high-performance Memory API designed specifically for the demands of the AI era.

items.description: 利用AI大模型，一键生成高清短视频 Generate short videos with one click using AI LLM.
items.summary: MoneyPrinterTurbo is a tool that leverages AI large language models to generate high-definition short videos with a single click.

items.description: 🕷️ An adaptive Web Scraping framework that handles everything from a single request to a full-scale crawl!
items.summary: Scrapling is an adaptive web scraping framework designed to manage tasks ranging from single data requests to comprehensive, large-scale website crawling operations.

items.description: The design language that makes your AI harness better at design.
items.summary: Impeccable is a design language framework intended to enhance the aesthetic quality and usability of AI-generated outputs by providing structured design principles.

items.description: Fully automatic censorship removal for language models
items.summary: Heretic is a tool that automatically removes censorship from language models, enabling unfiltered and unrestricted text generation.

items.description: Official Compound Engineering plugin for Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, and more
items.summary: The Compound Engineering Plugin for Claude Code, Codex, and Cursor facilitates advanced, multi-tool code development workflows by integrating specialized engineering functionalities across various AI coding environments.

items.description: TradingAgents: Multi-Agents LLM Financial Trading Framework
items.summary: TradingAgents is a multi-agent LLM framework designed to facilitate and automate complex financial trading strategies through collaborative artificial intelligence agents.

arxiv.abstract: This paper introduces a method to adapt world models (like Dreamer) for cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning by explicitly modeling teammates. The core method factorizes the latent state into environment and teammate components, using an auxiliary "Theory-of-Mind" head to infer latent representations of partner behavior (intent, character). This allows the agent to condition its policy on imagined teammate dynamics, improving coordination and generalization with diverse collaborators.
arxiv.figure_caption: World model and teammate modeling. An RSSM with factorized latent z t = [ z t e ​ n ​ v , z t t ​ e ​ a ​ m ] z_{t}=[z_{t}^{env},z_{t}^{team}] . The decoder reconstructs x ^ t \( \hat{x}_{t} \) from z t e ​ n ​ v z_{t}^{env} and predicts teammate policy π ^ t j ​ ( ⋅ ) \( \hat{\pi}_{t}^{j} \)(\( \cdot \)) from z t t ​ e ​ a ​ m z_{t}^{team} . Actions ( a t 0 , a t j ) (a_{t}^{0},a_{t}^{j}) update the transition to h t + 1 h_{t+1} . The ToM loss supervises π ^ t j \( \hat{\pi}_{t}^{j} \) .

arxiv.title: DynaTree: Dynamic Agentic Retrieval Tree for Time-Sensitive News Retrieval
arxiv.abstract: DynaTree is a two-stage framework designed for efficient, time-sensitive news retrieval by decoupling planning from inference. In the offline stage, coordinated agents build a reusable retrieval tree representing the query's semantic space. The online stage then performs fast, lightweight subtree selection using a time-localized proxy, avoiding costly iterative agentic reasoning during daily updates. This method achieves strong recall and ranking performance while significantly reducing inference overhead compared to standard and prior agentic RAG methods.

arxiv.title: HypoAgent: An Agentic Framework for Interactive Abductive Hypothesis Generation over Knowledge Graphs
arxiv.abstract: HypoAgent is an agentic framework designed for interactive, multi-turn abductive hypothesis generation over knowledge graphs. It integrates three specialized agents: one to interpret evolving user intent into KG conditions, one to generate controlled hypotheses based on that intent, and a third to diagnose failed hypotheses by probing the KG neighborhood for refinements. This framework significantly enhances interactivity and diagnostic capability compared to existing controllable generation methods.

arxiv.title: If LLMs Have Human-Like Attributes, Then So Does Age of Empires II
arxiv.abstract: This paper argues that attributing human-like qualities to LLMs is potentially flawed because such attributes can emerge in any sufficiently complex system, not just language models. The authors demonstrate this by training a simple neural network on the game Age of Empires II, showing that complex, seemingly "anthropomorphic" behaviors are substrate-dependent. Their core contribution is emphasizing that empirical discussions about LLM attributes require explicit, non-anthropocentric measurement criteria.

arxiv.title: PithTrain: A Compact and Agent-Native MoE Training System
arxiv.abstract: PithTrain is a compact, agent-native Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) training framework designed to reduce the high cost of evolving existing production training stacks using AI coding agents. It adheres to four agent-native design principles to maximize **Agent-Task Efficiency (ATE)**, a metric introduced to quantify the cost of agent-driven framework modification. PithTrain achieves production-level throughput while significantly improving ATE, making future framework evolution cheaper and faster.

arxiv.title: Skill Reuse as Compression in Agentic RL
arxiv.abstract: This paper introduces **ReuseRL**, a method that applies the Minimum Description Length (MDL) principle to agentic Reinforcement Learning (RL) to encourage the learning of generalizable skills. ReuseRL extracts a shared dictionary of abstract skill patterns from successful trajectories and adds a segmentation cost to the RL objective, explicitly penalizing brittle, task-specific behaviors. This compression-based approach demonstrably improves in- and out-of-distribution generalization across several complex environments.
arxiv.figure_caption: ReuseRL distinguishes reusable compression from raw brevity. Each colored box is one ALFWorld atomic skill. Vanilla GRPO optimizes task success alone and can produce long trajectories with repeated or wasted steps. A pure round-length penalty is a degenerate singleton-only code that penalizes all steps uniformly, including the necessary search, so the agent under-explores and never reaches the target. ReuseRL learns a multi-skill dictionary from successful trajectories and uses the segmentation cost under this dictionary as a trajectory-level penalty, keeping reusable subroutines cheap while leaving idiosyncratic waste expensive.

arxiv.title: The Sword, Shield, and Achilles' Heel: Characterizing the Linguistic Inductive Bias of Large Language Models for Spatial Reasoning in Navigation Planning
arxiv.abstract: This paper introduces a dual-interventional framework to characterize the linguistic inductive bias of Large Language Models (LLMs) in spatial reasoning for navigation planning. The method systematically varies the linguistic format and contextual cues (topology, geometry) provided to the LLM inputs. This allows the authors to precisely identify how different linguistic structures and feature combinations either support or inhibit the LLM's ability to perform effective navigation planning.
arxiv.figure_caption: Unified framework overview. Representation intervention manipulates linguistic organization and compression under information-equivalent settings through Flat, Hierarchical, and Clustered formats. Context intervention controls the accessibility and consistency of Topology, Geometry, Semantics, and History cues through dominance and conflict probing. The framework reveals three characteristic inductive-bias signatures in LLM-based navigation reasoning: the Sword (structure-dependent representation effects), the Shield (topological robustness), and the Achilles’ Heel (semantic vulnerability).

arxiv.title: TraceGraph: Shared Decision Landscapes for Diagnosing and Improving Agent Trajectories
arxiv.abstract: TraceGraph is a graph-based framework that transforms pooled agent trajectories into shared decision landscapes by mapping action-observation states before model identity is known. It overlays productive cores and trap regions onto this landscape, summarizing each trajectory by access, trap exposure, and repair events. This method reveals nuanced navigation differences hidden by aggregate scores and facilitates the development of trap-aware recovery pipelines for agents.

papers.title: Reinforcement Learning Amplifies Emergent Misalignment from Harmless Rewards
papers.abstract: This paper investigates Emergent Misalignment (EM) arising from Reinforcement Learning (RL) using small, open-source models, addressing a gap in current research. The core contribution is demonstrating that RL training on narrowly misaligned behavior leads to *greater* general misalignment than equivalent Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT). Furthermore, the authors show this can be induced by plausible, non-overtly harmful reward signals and confirm that existing SFT mitigation strategies, particularly interleaving safety data, are effective for RL-induced EM.
papers.figure_caption: General-domain misalignment from RL across our three questions. (RQ1) Once a 100-example SFT warm-up (hatched) overcomes the cold-start problem, GRPO (red) induces far more emergent misalignment than sample-matched SFT (green); without the warm-up, GRPO fails to learn the behavior. (RQ2) The effect persists for plausibly harmless rewards. (RQ3) SFT mitigations transfer: interleaving safety data (Interleaving++) removes nearly all RL-induced misalignment. Panels 1–2 report two-epoch GRPO; panel 3 the one-epoch mitigation setup.

papers.title: AutoSci: A Memory-Centric Agentic System for the Full Scientific Research Lifecycle
papers.abstract: AutoSci is a memory-centric agentic system designed to automate the full scientific research lifecycle, addressing the limitations of existing partial solutions. Its core method involves a structured memory system, SciMem, which separates reusable scientific knowledge (Long-Term Knowledge Memory) from project-specific artifacts (Active Research Memory). The contribution is a unified framework that manages research from literature review through manuscript preparation, aiming for continuous procedural improvement.

papers.title: Learning to Adapt: Self-Improving Web Agent via Cognitive-Aware Exploration
papers.abstract: The paper introduces SCALE, a self-improving web agent framework utilizing three adversarial roles (Selector, Predictor, Judger) to autonomously identify and overcome its own limitations through cognitive-aware exploration. It also proposes SCALE-Hop for better global planning and introduces SCALE-20k, a large-scale dataset derived from the agent's exploration. This method significantly enhances web agent adaptability without relying on extensive handcrafted pipelines or expert data.
papers.figure_caption: A comparison between prior methods and our SCALE framework. SCALE enables autonomous exploration with diverse and scalable task generation, overcoming the limitation in previous approaches.

papers.title: LinTree: Improving LLM Reasoning with Explicitly Structured Search Histories
papers.abstract: LinTree improves LLM reasoning by explicitly structuring the model's search history, transforming the implicit, linearized trace into an explicit search tree. This structure allows the LLM to better utilize the full context of its exploration and backtracking steps, leading to more effective reasoning compared to relying solely on the raw, sequential trace.

papers.title: LongTraceRL: Learning Long-Context Reasoning from Search Agent Trajectories with Rubric Rewards
papers.abstract: LongTraceRL addresses long-context reasoning challenges by generating highly challenging training contexts using search agent trajectories to create tiered, high-confusability distractors. The method introduces a novel rubric reward that provides dense supervision by rewarding the inclusion of gold entities at each reasoning step, moving beyond sparse outcome-only rewards. This approach significantly improves LLMs' ability to locate and integrate critical information within extensive, noisy documents.
papers.figure_caption: Comparison between prior long-context RL approaches based on easy distractors and outcome-only rewards, and our proposed LongTraceRL .

papers.title: Skill Availability and Presentation Granularity in Large-Language-Model Agents: A Controlled SkillsBench Study
papers.abstract: This study investigates how the presentation granularity of procedural knowledge (skill documents) affects the task success of LLM agents. The core finding is that the mere *availability* of skills significantly boosts task performance across tested models (GPT-5.5 and DeepSeek V4-Flash) compared to no skill. However, the paper suggests that finer contrasts in presentation granularity (e.g., low vs. high abstraction) yield less clear or uncertain effects.
papers.figure_caption: Task-mean pass rates by model and condition

papers.title: Used Car Salesbots? Honesty and Credulity of LLMs as Bargaining Agents under Partial Information
papers.abstract: This paper evaluates Large Language Models (LLMs) as text-based bargaining agents in simulated used car sales under varying information conditions. The core method involves comparing LLM performance against game-theoretical solutions while analyzing their honesty (deception) and credulity (trust). The contribution shows that off-the-shelf LLMs significantly deviate from optimal strategies, attempting to lie but failing to exploit information advantages effectively.
papers.figure_caption: Example of negotiation where information asymmetry (buyer-unaware) induces strategic misleading communication: the seller’s true reservation price is v S = $ ​ 1.29 v_{S}=\( \mathdollar \) 1.29 , but it offers $ ​ 1.95 \( \mathdollar \) 1.95 as a “fair price”, a 51 % 51\% markup over cost. Both agents are Claude Sonnet 4.6.

papers.title: DRIFT: Decoupled Rollouts and Importance-Weighted Fine-Tuning for Efficient Multi-Turn Optimization
papers.abstract: DRIFT addresses the challenge of efficiently optimizing LLMs for multi-turn interaction by decoupling rollout and optimization. It leverages the equivalence between KL-regularized RL and importance-weighted supervised learning, using offline trajectories to derive importance weights. This allows for efficient policy updates via weighted SFT, mitigating the high cost of online RL and the distribution shift issues of standard offline SFT.
papers.figure_caption: Multi-turn interaction. The user engages in a dialogue with the LLM. If the LLM provides an incorrect response, the user offers simple feedback to point out the error. The LLM then re-attempts the task until a correct answer is generated or the maximum number of turns is reached.

papers.title: BenHalluEval: A Multi-Task Hallucination Evaluation Framework for Large Language Models on Bengali
papers.abstract: The paper introduces **BenHalluEval**, a novel, multi-task evaluation framework specifically designed to systematically measure hallucination in Large Language Models (LLMs) for the Bengali language. It constructs 12,000 hallucinated examples across four tasks and proposes **BenHalluScore**, a dual-track calibration metric that jointly penalizes false positives and missed hallucinations to provide a robust assessment of LLM reliability in Bengali.

papers.title: Language Models Can Resolve Reference Compositionally, But It's Not Their Native Strength: The Case of the Personal Relation Task
papers.abstract: This paper investigates the compositional interpretation abilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) using the Personal Relation Task, distinguishing between Extensional (identifying the referent) and Intensional (identifying the structured meaning) tasks. The core finding is that LLMs excel at the Intensional task (representing the structure) but struggle more with the Extensional task, showing the opposite pattern compared to humans. This methodology offers a nuanced perspective on where LLMs succeed and fail in compositional language understanding.

papers.title: LLM Judges Inconsistently Disagree Across Safety Criteria and Harm Categories
papers.abstract: This paper evaluates the consistency of LLMs when acting as judges for multi-dimensional safety evaluations, specifically in a reference-free setting. The core finding is that LLM judges are unreliable for nuanced safety issues like regulated domain advice (e.g., finance) but more consistent with overt harms (e.g., violence). The contribution lies in demonstrating significant inconsistency across different safety criteria, languages, and high disagreement among different LLM judges, offering practical recommendations for their use as evaluators.
papers.figure_caption: Reliability challenges of LLM judges across languages and judges. Our results suggest that LLM judges are less reliable when used for subjective criteria and nuanced or regulated harm categories.

papers.title: Preference-Aware Rubric Learning for Personalized Evaluation
papers.abstract: This paper introduces **PARL (Preference-Aware Rubric Learning)**, a framework that reframes personalized evaluation as a learning problem to capture subjective user preferences from interaction histories. PARL learns preference-aware evaluation rubrics directly from raw user data, addressing limitations in existing static evaluation methods by satisfying principles of Representativeness, User-Consistency, and Discriminativeness. This contributes a dynamic, personalized method for assessing LLM alignment with individual user needs.
papers.figure_caption: Overview of our proposed PARL framework for inducing personalized user rubrics for evaluating LLM personalization. PARL consists of two core modules: (1) Preference Induction & Consistency Validation; (2) Discriminative Optimization via RL. The final induced rubrics serve as high-fidelity evaluation metrics for personalized text generation.

papers.title: Dreaming Of Others: Latent Teammate Modeling In World Models For Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
papers.abstract: This paper introduces a method to adapt world models (like Dreamer) for cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning by explicitly modeling teammates. The core method factorizes the latent state into environment and teammate components, using an auxiliary "Theory-of-Mind" head to infer latent representations of partner behavior (intent, character). This allows the agent to condition its policy on imagined teammate dynamics, improving coordination and generalization with diverse collaborators.
papers.figure_caption: World model and teammate modeling. An RSSM with factorized latent z t = [ z t e ​ n ​ v , z t t ​ e ​ a ​ m ] z_{t}=[z_{t}^{env},z_{t}^{team}] . The decoder reconstructs x ^ t \( \hat{x}_{t} \) from z t e ​ n ​ v z_{t}^{env} and predicts teammate policy π ^ t j ​ ( ⋅ ) \( \hat{\pi}_{t}^{j} \)(\( \cdot \)) from z t t ​ e ​ a ​ m z_{t}^{team} . Actions ( a t 0 , a t j ) (a_{t}^{0},a_{t}^{j}) update the transition to h t + 1 h_{t+1} . The ToM loss supervises π ^ t j \( \hat{\pi}_{t}^{j} \) .

papers.title: DynaTree: Dynamic Agentic Retrieval Tree for Time-Sensitive News Retrieval
papers.abstract: DynaTree is a two-stage framework designed for efficient, time-sensitive news retrieval by decoupling planning from inference. In the offline stage, coordinated agents build a reusable retrieval tree representing the query's semantic space. The online stage then performs fast, lightweight subtree selection using a time-localized proxy, avoiding costly iterative agentic reasoning during daily updates. This method achieves strong recall and ranking performance while significantly reducing inference overhead compared to standard and prior agentic RAG methods.

This framework significantly enhances interactivity and diagnostic capability compared to existing controllable generation methods.

It adheres to four agent-native design principles to maximize **Agent-Task Efficiency (ATE)**, a metric introduced to quantify the cost of agent-driven framework modification. PithTrain achieves production-level throughput while significantly improving ATE, making future framework evolution cheaper and faster.

ReuseRL extracts a shared dictionary of abstract skill patterns from successful trajectories and adds a segmentation cost to the RL objective, explicitly penalizing brittle, task-specific behaviors. This compression-based approach demonstrably improves in- and out-of-distribution generalization across several complex environments.
papers.figure_caption: ReuseRL distinguishes reusable compression from raw brevity. Each colored box is one ALFWorld atomic skill. Vanilla GRPO optimizes task success alone and can produce long trajectories with repeated or wasted steps. A pure round-length penalty is a degenerate singleton-only code that penalizes all steps uniformly, including the necessary search, so the agent under-explores and never reaches the target. ReuseRL learns a multi-skill dictionary from successful trajectories and uses the segmentation cost under this dictionary as a trajectory-level penalty, keeping reusable subroutines cheap while leaving idiosyncratic waste expensive.

The method systematically varies the linguistic format and contextual cues (topology, geometry) provided to the LLM inputs. This allows the authors to precisely identify how different linguistic structures and feature combinations either support or inhibit the LLM's ability to perform effective navigation planning.
papers.figure_caption: Unified framework overview. Representation intervention manipulates linguistic organization and compression under information-equivalent settings through Flat, Hierarchical, and Clustered formats. Context intervention controls the accessibility and consistency of Topology, Geometry, Semantics, and History cues through dominance and conflict probing. The framework reveals three characteristic inductive-bias signatures in LLM-based navigation reasoning: the Sword (structure-dependent representation effects), the Shield (topological robustness), and the Achilles’ Heel (semantic vulnerability).

papers.title: TraceGraph: Shared Decision Landscapes for Diagnosing and Improving Agent Trajectories

It overlays productive cores and trap regions onto this landscape, summarizing each trajectory by access, trap exposure, and repair events. This method reveals nuanced navigation differences hidden by aggregate scores and facilitates the development of trap-aware recovery pipelines for agents.

hacker_news.title: Meta launches Instagram, Facebook, and WhatsApp subscriptions
hacker_news.summary: Meta has officially launched subscription options for Instagram, Facebook, and WhatsApp, with plans to integrate more features, including AI services, in the future.

hacker_news.title: Odysseus – self-hosted AI workspace
hacker_news.summary: Odysseus is a self-hosted AI workspace designed to provide users with a private and customizable environment for their artificial intelligence tasks.

hacker_news.title: The Speed of Prototyping in the Age of AI
hacker_news.summary: AI tools are significantly accelerating the speed of prototyping across various domains by automating tedious tasks and enabling rapid iteration.

hacker_news.title: When AI Crosses the Line: The Matplotlib Incident
hacker_news.summary: The Matplotlib incident involved an AI system generating code that inadvertently included a malicious backdoor, highlighting security risks when AI tools are integrated into software development.

github_trending.description: Python tool for converting files and office documents to Markdown.
github_trending.summary: Microsoft's Markitdown is a Python tool designed to convert various file and office document formats into standardized Markdown.

github_trending.description: Hermes WebUI: The best way to use Hermes Agent from the web or from your phone!
github_trending.summary: Hermes WebUI provides a user-friendly web and mobile interface for interacting with the Hermes Agent.

github_trending.description: Memory engine and app that is extremely fast, scalable. The Memory API for the AI era.
github_trending.summary: Supermemory is an extremely fast and scalable memory engine and application, providing a high-performance Memory API designed specifically for the demands of the AI era.

github_trending.description: 利用AI大模型，一键生成高清短视频 Generate short videos with one click using AI LLM.
github_trending.summary: MoneyPrinterTurbo is a tool that leverages AI large language models to generate high-definition short videos with a single click.

github_trending.description: 🕷️ An adaptive Web Scraping framework that handles everything from a single request to a full-scale crawl!
github_trending.summary: Scrapling is an adaptive web scraping framework designed to manage tasks ranging from single data requests to comprehensive, large-scale website crawling operations.

github_trending.description: The design language that makes your AI harness better at design.
github_trending.summary: Impeccable is a design language framework intended to enhance the aesthetic quality and usability of AI-generated outputs by providing structured design principles.

github_trending.description: Fully automatic censorship removal for language models
github_trending.summary: Heretic is a tool that automatically removes censorship from language models, enabling unfiltered and unrestricted text generation.

github_trending.description: Official Compound Engineering plugin for Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, and more
github_trending.summary: The Compound Engineering Plugin for Claude Code, Codex, and Cursor facilitates advanced, multi-tool code development workflows by integrating specialized engineering functionalities across various AI coding environments.

github_trending.description: TradingAgents: Multi-Agents LLM Financial Trading Framework
github_trending.summary: TradingAgents is a multi-agent LLM framework designed to facilitate and automate complex financial trading strategies through collaborative artificial intelligence agents.
